content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

November 28, 1995


Scott D. Alevy
Nelson Communications Group
501 West Broadway, Suite 2020
San Diego, CA 92101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-95-368

Dear Mr. Alevy:

This is in response to your request for advice as a member of
the Chula Vista City Council under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/ The facts set out in
this letter were provided in both your written request for advice,
as well as in our November 7, 1995, telephone conversation.

QUESTION

May you participate in deliberations and vote on issues
pertaining to the Baldwin Company, a client of your employer,
regarding the Baldwin Company's planned development in the City of
Chula Vista?

CONCLUSION

If Nelson doesn't intend to bid to do work in connection with
the Chula Vista project, it is not foreseeable that the Baldwin
Company's Chula Vista development will have a material financial
effect on your employer, and you may participate in these
decisions.


1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18995. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Advice File No. A-95-368
Page 2


FACTS

You are an appointed member of the Chula Vista City Council.
You are also a salaried employee in the San Diego office of Nelson
Communications Group ("Nelson"). You do not have a partnership
interest in Nelson, which is a privately-held corporation. Nelson
is a public relations firm which, among other things, works for
developer/clients in moving proposed developments through the city
and county planning and approval process.

Pending before the city council is a proposed development
planned by the Baldwin Company. Nelson's Irvine office is
currently performing media relations work for the Baldwin
Company's parent company. The work is related to the parent
company's bankruptcy proceedings and is not part of the
development before the Chula Vista City Council.

ANALYSIS

No public official at any level of state or local
governmental shall make, participate in making, or in any way
attempt to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to
know he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.)

An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate
family, or on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

* * *

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status, aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

* * *

For purposes of this section, indirect investment
or interest means any investment or interest owned by

Advice File No. A-95-368
Page 3


the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by
an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a
business entity or trust in which the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent
interest or greater.

(Section 87103(a),(c) and (d).)

"Income" means any payment received, including but not
limited to any salary, wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent,
proceeds from any sale, gift or loan. Income of an individual
also includes a pro rata share of any income of any business
entity or trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater.
(Section 82030.)

You indicated that you do not have a partnership interest in
Nelson, but rather you are a salaried employee. You do not have
an investment interest in Nelson as used in Section 87103(a),
since you do not have a 10 percent or greater interest in Nelson,
the Baldwin Company is thus not an economic interest to you.

Accordingly, as a public official, you may not make,
participate in making, or use your official position to influence
any decisions which may have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on your employer, Nelson Communications. (Section
87103(d).)

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are made
reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case. An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

In your letter, you stated that Nelson's San Diego Office,
where you are employed, is not presently representing the Baldwin
Company on the Chula Vista development and none is anticipated.
In fact, during our telephone conversation, you indicated that
Baldwin traditionally bids out its media relations work.
You indicated that should Baldwin request bids from firms on this
project, several public relations firms in the area would probably
bid on such a contract.

The general rule in In re Thorner (supra) is that where the
business entity in which the official has an economic interest
makes a bid on a contract or is preparing to make a bid, a
financial effect on the business entity is reasonably foreseeable
even if there is substantial competition. Thus, for example, if
the city council's decision provides the impetus for Baldwin to
request bids on the Chula Vista development and Nelson plans on

Advice File No. A-95-368
Page 4


submitting a bid for the public relations work in connection with
the project, it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a financial effect on Nelson.

If Nelson does not intend to bid on the Chula Vista project,
it does not appear reasonably foreseeable that the project will have a
material financial effect on Nelson. You would thus not be
prohibited from participating in this decision.

If, however, at any time during the process, it becomes
apparent that there is a substantial likelihood that Nelson may
bid on the Baldwin contract, consistent with Thorou, you would be
required to disqualify yourself from participating in Chula Vista
development decisions if there is a "nexus" between the purpose
for which you receive income and the governmental decision, or if
it were reasonably foreseeable that the decisions would have an
indirect effect on Nelson.

A "nexus" exists if you were to receive income to achieve a
goal or purpose which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or
hindered by the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.1(d).)
In other words, you may not accomplish in your public capacity
what you are paid to accomplish in your private capacity.
(Nelson Advice Letter No. I-91-443.) Accordingly, if Nelson
compensates you to lobby or promote the Baldwin Chula Vista
project before the city, "nexus" applies and you may not
participate in your governmental capacity on Chula Vista
development decisions.

If "nexus" as described above does not apply, you may still
be disqualified from participating in your governmental capacity
if Nelson will be indirectly affected by the decision.

You indicated that Nelson is a privately-held corporation
which is not traded on any stock exchange. Accordingly, assuming
that subsection (g) of Regulation 18702.2 applies, a decision would
be deemed to have a "material financial effect" on Nelson if the
decision:

(1) Will result in an increase or decrease in
Nelson's gross revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000 or
more; or

(2) Will result in Nelson incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500 or
more; or

(3) Will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of Nelson's assets or liabilities of $10,000 or
more.

(Regulation 18702.2(g).)

Advice File No. A-95-368
Page 5


Accordingly, provided that Nelson does not intend to bid on
the Baldwin contract, it is not foreseeable that Nelson will be
affected by decisions on the Chula Vista development. However,
should Nelson decide to bid on the project, and Nelson compensate
you to lobby or promote the Chula Vista project, "nexus" applies
and you may not participate in these decisions. Additionally, If
"nexus" does not apply, you could not participate if Nelson will
be indirectly affected by the project in any of the monetary
amounts specified above.

I trust this answers your questions sufficiently. If you
have additional questions, or need to discuss this, please feel
free to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

SGC/JET/jt
